coredata.dc:title: Regulating lithium metal interface using seed-coating layer for high-power batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Ponraj, Rubha
Yun, Jong Hyuk
Wang, Ji Eun
Chen, Xianjue
Kim, Dong Jun
Kim, Do Kyung
coredata.dc:description: Li metal has received heightened attention as an anode material for next-generation batteries due to its extremely large theoretical specific capacity. Employing Li metal anodes, however, involves considerable challenges, such as the formation of Li dendrites, depletion of electrolyte, and breakage of the solid electrolyte interphase layer. These issues potentially limit cell cycle life and pose safety risks. While recent studies demonstrated promising results, facile and scalable methods for achieving high power Li metal batteries are still elusive. Herein, the interface of lithium metal is regulated by seed-coating of nitrogen-rich graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), which facilitates the formation of a robust and stable solid electrolyte interface layer. The highly lithiophilic nature of N-GQDs enables uniform lithium ionic flux even at high current densities and promotes dendrite-free lithium plating morphology with a low nucleation overpotential of 3 mV (45 mV for bare copper). Such a robust layer enables highly reversible Li plating/stripping in symmetric cell configuration for over 8,000 h at a current density of 30 mA cm−2. Furthermore, the lithium iron phosphate-coupled full cell performs stable cycling for over 500 cycles. The introduction of seed layers represents a viable strategy for achieving high-power Li metal batteries by regulating the highly reactive lithium metal surface.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Lithium metal anode
Lithium metal batteries
Seed-coating
Solid Electrolyte Interface

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: N-GQD Synthesis and seed-coating on 3D Cu foam
Nucleation of Li on the seed-coated N-GQD substrate
Long-term and high current stability of N-GQD@Cu electrode
Understanding the effect of N-GQD seed-coating

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis process of N-GQD. (b) N-GQD solution under UV light and high-resolution TEM images of N-GQDs with an inset showing a single N-GQD particle. (c) XPS survey spectrum, showing the composition of N, C, and O for the synthesized N-GQDs and high-resolution XPS spectra of (d) C 1 s, (e) O 1 s, and (f) N 1 s. (g) FTIR spectra of carbonized hemp (black line) and N-GQDs (green line). (h) A schematic representing the chemical structure of the synthesized N-GQD. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) The illustration of seed-coating process of N-GQD on 3D Cu foam substrate. (b) Voltage profiles of the first Li plating/stripping cycle on Cu-foam and N-GQD@Cu. (c) Coulombic efficiency of Li plating/stripping on Cu-foam and N-GQD@Cu with an areal capacity of 1 mAh cm−2 at current densities of 2 mA cm−2 and 5 mA cm−2.

SEM images of Cu-foam after (a-b) 1 mAh cm−2 of Li plating and (c-d) after Li stripping, showing dendrite formation on the surface of Cu-foam. SEM images of N-GQD@Cu after (e-f) 1 mAh cm−2 of Li plating and (g-h) after Li stripping, showing smooth surface aided by the N-GQD coating.

(a) Li plating/stripping cycling of Cu|Cu and N-GQD@Cu|N-GQD@Cu symmetric cells under a current density of 1, 5, and 10 mA cm−2 followed by continuous cycling at 20 mA cm−2 with an areal capacity of 1 mAh cm−2 for 9,000 h with a constant overpotential of ∼12.6 mV. Detailed results of the initial 10 hours of (b) Cu|Cu and (c) N-GQD@Cu|N-GQD@Cu cells. (d) Cu|Cu and N-GQD@Cu|N-GQD@Cu symmetric cell cycling with a high current density of 20 mA cm−2 to 5 mAh cm−2 capacity. (e) Li plating/stripping cycling and voltage profiles of N-GQD@Cu|N-GQD@Cu symmetric cells with an areal capacity of 1 mAh cm−2 at a current density of 30 mA cm−2.

(a) A representative FIB-TEM cross-sectional image of Li-plated N-GQD@Cu electrode with a thickness of ∼50 nm Li deposition on Cu-foam underneath ∼80 nm of N-GQD/SEI layer. (b) Design of capillary half-cell for in-operando optical microscopy observation. c) Schematic illustration of N-GQD-coated Cu wire before and after Li deposition at a current density of 5 mA cm−2. High-resolution depth profiles of (d) F 1 s and (e) Li 1 s XPS spectra of Li-plated N-GQD@Cu electrode.

(a) Rate capability of pre-lithiated N-GQD@Cu |LFP and Cu|LFP full cells under 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, and 0.5C rates. (b) Cycling performance of rate capability test cells at 1.0C rate. (c) Charge/discharge profile of pre-lithiated N-GQD@Cu |LFP full cell under 0.1Crate.

head.ce:title: Regulating lithium metal interface using seed-coating layer for high-power batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST), 291 Daehak-ro, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon 34141, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST), 291 Daehak-ro, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon 34141, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, The University of New South Wales, Sydney, NSW 2052, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The University of New South Wales

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, The University of New South Wales, High St, Kensington, NSW 2052, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Life Sciences, The University of Newcastle, Callaghan, NSW 2308, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Life Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental and Life Sciences, The University of Newcastle, Callaghan, NSW 2308, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Li metal has received heightened attention as an anode material for next-generation batteries due to its extremely large theoretical specific capacity. Employing Li metal anodes, however, involves considerable challenges, such as the formation of Li dendrites, depletion of electrolyte, and breakage of the solid electrolyte interphase layer. These issues potentially limit cell cycle life and pose safety risks. While recent studies demonstrated promising results, facile and scalable methods for achieving high power Li metal batteries are still elusive. Herein, the interface of lithium metal is regulated by seed-coating of nitrogen-rich graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), which facilitates the formation of a robust and stable solid electrolyte interface layer. The highly lithiophilic nature of N-GQDs enables uniform lithium ionic flux even at high current densities and promotes dendrite-free lithium plating morphology with a low nucleation overpotential of 3 mV (45 mV for bare copper). Such a robust layer enables highly reversible Li plating/stripping in symmetric cell configuration for over 8,000 h at a current density of 30 mA cm−2. Furthermore, the lithium iron phosphate-coupled full cell performs stable cycling for over 500 cycles. The introduction of seed layers represents a viable strategy for achieving high-power Li metal batteries by regulating the highly reactive lithium metal surface.

ce:section.ce:para: The global transition away from fossil fuels has prompted the increasing usage of Li-ion batteries (LIBs) in automobiles and energy grids [1–3]. Despite the successful implementation of LIBs in small electronic devices, significant advances in their electrochemical performance are required to satisfy the high standards of large-scale energy storage [3]. In particular, the limited capacity of graphite anode material is one of the reasons, hindering the improvement of energy density in LIBs [2,4]. Among the available electrode candidates, Li metal is receiving attention as a promising anode to replace conventional graphite. Li metal presents an exceptionally high theoretical capacity of 3860 mAh g−1 and a low redox potential of −3.04 V (vs. standard hydrogen electrode), rendering it an excellent anode candidate [5–7].

The major challenge in employing Li metal anode is achieving a stable interface between Li metal and the liquid electrolyte [8]. Throughout the redox processes, Li metal inherently undergoes irreversible side reactions limiting battery cycle life and creating potential fire safety hazards [9–12]. In detail, electrolyte spontaneously decomposes at the anode–electrolyte interface, resulting in the depletion of electrolyte. Moreover, surface heterogeneities on the electrode/electrolyte interface induce Li dendrite formation, which can trigger thermal runaway or pose safety hazards [10,13,14]. To overcome these challenges, the highly reactive nature of Li metal should be restrained and recent reports have shown promising outcomes. For example, 3D metal- [15–17] and carbon-based host structures [18–20] delayed and accommodated the growth of Li dendrites during battery cycling. In addition, the use of functional coating layers, such as zinc oxide [21,22], polydopamine [23], and N- and heteroatom-doped graphene [24–27], have been effective for promoting uniform Li nucleation and suppressing Li dendrite formation [22]. From the electrolyte perspective, using super-concentrated electrolytes [28,29], additives [30,31], and dual-salt configurations [28,32] have minimized the formation of dendritic structures in Li metal anodes. In addition to these approaches, self-heating-induced dendrite healing and dendrite suppression through sinusoidal ripple current have been explored [14,33]. Despite the recent progress in mitigating Li dendrite formation, the critical problem concerning the uncontrollable volume change of Li metal during cycling has not been largely satisfied. The undesired volume changes induce the breakage of critical and vulnerable solid electrolyte interface (SEI) layer, resulting in the formation of dead Li and Li dendrites. Moreover, considering that next-generation applications require much higher current densities compared with portable electronic devices, the challenges related to unstable SEI layers are further amplified. Therefore, it is critical to design a suitable electrode structure that can deposit and dissolve Li metal flawlessly and maintain a stable SEI layer under high-power operating conditions.

Herein, we report the scalable synthesis of nitrogen-rich graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and their use as a seed-coating layer in Li metal batteries (LMBs). The carbonization of hemp fibers followed by a hydrothermal reaction produced 2–3 nm-sized N-GQDs with a remarkably high yield of 55%. Thereafter, N-GQDs were seed-coated on 3D Cu foam and used as a current collector for LMBs. During the first charge–discharge cycle, the N-GQD layer between the Cu foam and electrolyte induced a homogeneous and exceedingly stable SEI layer. The mechanically robust SEI layer featured an extremely low nucleation overpotential, dendrite-free Li plating, and stable cycling even at a current density of 30 mA cm−2. Furthermore, Li metal plating/stripping occurred exclusively beneath the N-GQD layer on account of its combined lithiophilic and insulating characteristics. Consequently, the introduction of a facile seed-coating method represents a promising starting point for fabricating high-power LMBs aiming at practical applications.

Hemp fiber used in this work as a starting material for carbonization was obtained from Hemp Traders (Paramount, CA, USA). The as-received hemp fiber (0.250 g) dispersed in 70 ml of 2 M H2SO4 (Sigma Aldrich) solution was carbonized by hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 18 h [34]. The carbonized hemp fibers were washed with water and then dried. The carbonized hemp fiber (0.150 g) was dispersed in a solution mixture of 3 ml of 30 wt% H2O2 and 0.5 ml of 1,2-diaminoethane (Sigma Aldrich) in 45 ml DI water, and then hydrothermally treated at 180 °C for 40 min. The resulting product was then filtered through polytetrafluoroethylene membrane, and the filtrate was dialyzed in 1,000 Da dialysis bag for 2 h. Finally, the solution was freeze-dried to obtain N-GQDs. The as-synthesized N-GQDs were dissolved in DI water with a specific concentration. Cu foams were cleaned using acetone and ethanol and dried. Pre-weighed Cu foams were dipped inside the N-GQDs solution and vacuum dried, and then used for electrochemical plating and stripping.

The as-prepared N-GQD sample was dispersed in water and UV–visible absorption spectra were recorded on aPerkin Elmer Lambda 1050 spectrophotometer in the wavelength range of 250 nm to 800 nm. N-GQD particle size analysis was carried out using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM), and its chemical nature was characterized using Thermo VG Scientific X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) at ambient temperature. The morphology of N-GQD@Cu was observed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Morphological and elemental analysis of the lithium plated electrodes after cycling were carried out after a gentle wash with anhydrous DME solvent to remove the remnant electrolyte salt. The samples were dried in a glovebox to remove the residual solvent and then sealed in an argon-filled flask before transferring to the analyses chambers. Lithium plated N-GQD@Cu foam electrode was sectioned using focused ion beam (FIB) milling and characterized using HR-TEM. A customized transparentoperandocell glass tube was utilized for optical observation of lithium plating, where lithium coated copper wire was used as counter electrode and a thick layer of N-GQD coated copper wire was used as a working electrode. This cell tube was assembled with the electrolyte and was sealed inside the glovebox. Live videos and images of lithium plating and stripping were acquired by an optical microscope (Olympus BX51M). The cell was connected with the portable Bio-logic equipment to test its electrochemical performance.

The electrochemical properties of N-GQD coated electrodes were evaluated with CR2032 coin cells (MTI Corporation), which were fabricated inside a recirculating argon glove box. Lithium metal foil (Alfa Aesar) with a diameter of 14 mm was used as the counter electrode. The electrolyte (60 µl) for each half cell and symmetric cell was a solution mixture of 1 M LiTFSI salt and LiNO3 additive in DOL/DME (1:1 by volume). Polypropylene (PP, diameter: 18 mm, Celgard Inc.) membrane was used as the separator. The cells’ performance was investigated in a galvanostatic mode at various current densities using a WBS3000 battery cycler (Wonatech. Co.). Coulombic efficiency (CE) of the cells was calculated as ratio of lithium stripping capacity to plating capacity. Symmetric cells were assembled, in which, lithiated N-GQD@Cu was coupled with another lithiated N-GQD@Cu. Similarly, control symmetric cells were also prepared using lithium foils and lithiated bare copper foams. In case of cells with 1 mAh cm−2 target capacity, each electrode of the symmetric cell was pre-lithiated to 1 mAh cm−2. The LFP electrodes were fabricated with 75% active materials, 10% polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) and 15% carbon black. The active mass loadings of LFP electrodes were ∼5.0 mg cm−2. The current rates for LFP are based on their practical specific capacities of 170 mAh g−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-GQD Synthesis and seed-coating on 3D Cu foam
ce:section.ce:para: In general, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are graphene fragments with a lateral size of <100 nm. The nanometer-scale dimensions of GQDs confer on them quantum confinement effects, and the bandgap of GQDs can be tuned by changing the particle size or via heteroatom doping. GQDs are multifunctional materials with unique chemical and physical properties, and their applications in electronics, nanomedicine, and energy harvesting have been studied extensively [35–38].

In this study, we synthesized N-GQDs by carbonizing hemp fibers at 180 °C for 1 h to remove surface lignin, followed by hydrothermal reaction in the presence of sulfuric acid, hydrogen peroxide, and ethylenediamine (Fig. 1 a). Hemp fiber was chosen due to its abundant availability, low cost, high yield and sustainable nature. Ethylenediamine was added to incorporate nitrogen heteroatoms in GQDs. In addition, ethylenediamine rendered the reaction mixture basic nature and facilitated the formation of highly reactive hydroxyl radicals from hydrogen peroxide. The free radicals cleaved the bonds at the defective sites of the carbonized hemp fibers, resulting in a high yield (55%) of N-GQDs, which were homogeneously dispersed in water. The ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrum of N-GQDs showed a sharp peak in the wavelength range of 270–300 nm with a tail extending to 390 nm, which is a characteristic band indicating the polyaromatic structure and n–π* transitions of graphene (Fig. S1). Upon exposure to 365 nm light, N-GQDs emitted blue light (Fig. 1b). This was attributed to the combined effects of quantum confinement and blocking of the edge-site functional groups [39]. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of N-GQDs (Fig. 1b) revealed their uniform particle size distribution (<5 nm) and the lattice spacing of N-GQDs (∼0.35 nm) corresponds to the (002) plane of graphite.

The chemical composition of N-GQDs was analyzed using XPS, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and combustion elemental analysis. The XPS survey scan of N-GQDs (Fig. 1c) revealed that the N-GQDs were comprised of C, N, and O atoms. The high-resolution C1s XPS profile of N-GQDs was deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.6, 285.9, and 287.9 eV (Fig. 1d). The peak at 284.6 eV was assigned to sp2-hybridized C and the peak at 285.9 eV originated from the C–N and/or C–O groups, indicating the covalent bonds between N and/or O atoms and C atoms. The peaks at 287.9 and 530.9 eV in the C1s and O1s XPS profiles of N-GQDs were assigned to the C = O (carbonyl and carboxyl groups), respectively (Fig. 1d-e). The peak at 532 eV in the O1s XPS profile of N-GQDs was assigned to the C–OH and/or C–O–C bonds, confirming the presence of hydroxyl and epoxy groups in N-GQDs. The main peaks at 397.6, 399.8, and 401.5 eV in the N1s XPS profile of N-GQDs were attributed to pyridinic- (77 at.%), pyrrolic- (18 at.%), and graphitic-N species (5 at.%), respectively (Fig. 1f). Furthermore, FTIR spectra of N-GQDs revealed the presence of lithiophilic carbonyl, carboxyl, hydroxyl, and N-containing functional groups (Fig. 1g) [40]. Based on these results, a schematic representing the chemical structure of N-GQDs is presented in Fig. 1h. Combustion elemental analysis indicated that N-GQDs consisted of C (45.4 wt%), N (23.4 wt%), and O (23.5 wt%). The relatively large fraction of heteroatoms was associated with the presence of numerous functional groups on the surface of N-GQDs. It was expected that the abundant lithiophilic functional groups of N-GQDs could attract Li ions from the electrolyte and serve as an “ionic reservoir,” enabling a uniform Li-ion flux on the Li metal surface even at high current densities. Further details are included in Section 3.2.1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nucleation of Li on the seed-coated N-GQD substrate
ce:section.ce:para: The instability of the SEI layer of LMBs has been mainly attributed to the infinite volume changes during Li plating/stripping process. The repetitive volume changes induce the fracture of the SEI layer, resulting in the direct exposure of Li and consumption of the electrolyte. To address inhomogeneous nucleation of Li and mitigate the side effects of repetitive volume changes, we seed-coated N-GQDs on 3D Cu foam (N-GQD@Cu) and used it as a Li host for LMBs (Fig. 2 a). Herein, 3D Cu foam was chosen as the substrate to accommodate the volume change during lithium plating/stripping process and minimize the effective current density[41,42]. The detailed electrode fabrication procedure is described in Section 2.1. As demonstrated in previous studies, the good wettability of the electrolyte on the electrode surface is a prerequisite for achieving dendrite-free Li deposition. The contact angles of electrolyte on Cu-foam and N-GQD@Cu are 124° and 0° (complete wetting), respectively. (Fig. S2). The N-GQD seed-coating significantly improved the wettability of electrolyte on the Cu foam current collector due to the hydrophilic functional groups of N-GQDs. Additionally, the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the N-GQD@Cu revealed that the morphology of Cu foam substrate remained unchanged after N-GQD coating (Fig. S3a-b).

The N-GQD seed-coating also had a noticeable effect on the nucleation overpotential of Li. Half-cells were assembled with N-GQD@Cu or Cu foam as the working electrode, while Li metal as both the counter and reference electrodes. At a current density of 2 mA cm−2, the N-GQD@Cu electrode with an areal capacity of 1 mAh cm−2 presented an extremely low nucleation overpotential of 3 mV (Fig. 2b). By contrast, the nucleation overpotential of the control Cu foam electrode under the same conditions was significantly higher (45 mV). The facile nucleation of Li+ ions suggests that the N-GQD coating played a crucial role in improving the electrolyte wettability and lithiophilic characteristics.

The initial CEs of N-GQD@Cu and Cu foam electrodes were 61% and 73%, respectively (Fig. 2c). The relatively low initial CE of the N-GQD@Cu electrode was due to the high surface area of the N-GQD@Cu electrode and can be resolved by pre-lithiation strategies [43,44]. After the stabilization of SEI layer, the N-GQD@Cu electrode presented an extremely stable cycling performance, with an average CE of ∼ 97% during 400 cycles. In contrast, CE of the Cu foam started to fade after 120 cycles under 2 mA cm−2 and 70 cycles under 5 mA cm−2 current density. Since the only difference between the aforementioned electrodes was the N-GQD coating layer, the improved cycle life reflects the seed-coating stabilized the SEI layer and enhanced its durability.

The depletion of Li ions and formation of Li dendritic structures can be described using Sand’s time (τ), which is inversely proportional to the square of the effective current density and directly proportional to the diffusion coefficient (Eq. (2)) [45,46].
(2)
τ s
= π D
C 0
e
Z c
2 J
2
μ a
+
μ c
μ a
2

Where τ
s - time once dendrites start to grow,
D – Diffusion coefficient,

C
0 – initial concentration of salt,

Z
c – charge number of cations,
J – effective current density, and

µ
a and µ
c - anionic and cationic mobility, respectively.

Therefore, to extend Sand's time, the effective current density should be minimized, and the diffusion coefficient needs to be improved. Our findings show that the N-GQD coating results in a thin and stable SEI layer that induces uniform Li-ion flux and fast ionic conduction near the electrode surface. It is postulated that the lone electron pairs of the pyridinic-N atoms of N-GQD possibly act as Lewis bases and attract the Lewis-acidic Li+ ions in the electrolyte[47]. In addition, the electron-rich carbonyl groups of N-GQD can attract Li+ ions in the electrolyte via secondary interactions. The presence of such lithiophilic functional groups on the N-GQD surface attracted the Li ions in the electrolyte to serve as a direct or indirect Li-ion reservoir. Consequently, the homogeneous Li-ion flux on the Cu foam surface translated into uniform Li nucleation, which is reflected by the low nucleation overpotential of N-GQD@Cu. By contrast, the lack of lithiophilic functional groups on the Cu foam surface led to an inconsistent Li accumulation, thus generating Li dendrites.

Our hypothesis was further verified using SEM images of samples after the Li plating and stripping process. The Cu-foam electrode after 1 mAh cm−2 of Li plating exhibited inhomogeneous Li plating and Li dendrite formation (Fig. 3 a-b). After Li stripping, the SEM image revealed the presence of residual Li on the Cu-foam surface, indicating the formation of dead Li (Fig. 3c-d). In contrast, the SEM images of N-GQD@Cu displayed a smooth morphology without any presence of lithium dendrites and dead Li after Li plating and stripping, respectively (Fig. 3e-h). Moreover, SEM images of N-GQD@Cu electrode after 50 (Fig. S3c-d) and 100 cycles (Fig. S3e-f) of lithium plating/stripping at 2 mA cm−2 to a capacity of 1 mAh cm−2 exhibited smooth morphology with no visible dendrite formation. Overall, our findings confirm the importance of N-GQD seed-coating, which enables a dendrite-free and smooth Li plating process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Long-term and high current stability of N-GQD@Cu electrode
ce:section.ce:para: To better understand the effectiveness of N-GQD seed-coating, Li foil, pre-lithiated Cu-foam, and pre-lithiated N-GQD@Cu electrodes were used for the symmetric cell assembly and performed Li plating/stripping analysis. Initially, we optimized the amount of N-GQD coating to 4–5 mg (Fig. S4a) and the detailed optimization procedure is described in the Experimental Section. The voltage profiles of all three symmetric cells with an areal capacity of 1 mAh cm−2 cycled at a current density of 1 mA cm−2 are presented in Fig. S4b. The Li foil symmetric cell exhibited a gradual increase in Li plating/stripping overpotential up to 250 h, followed by voltage fluctuations and sudden cell failure. The increase in overpotential with cycling indicated a continuous breakage and formation of the SEI, which consequently thickened the insulating layers[48]. In addition, the voltage fluctuations prior to sudden cell failure presumably occurred due to the penetration of Li dendrites or micro-short-circuiting[9,49]. The cycling stability of the Cu foam symmetric cell increased up to 750 h, and cell failure occurred subsequently. The difference in lifetime is explained by the larger surface area of the Cu foam cell than that of the Li foil cell, thereby increasing the probability of accommodating the Li dendrites. Remarkably, the N-GQD@Cu symmetric cell exhibited excellent cycle life and stability and did not experience voltage fluctuations or failure even after 2,000 h of cycling. Moreover, the overpotential of the N-GQD@Cu symmetric cell was the lowest among all the tested cells. These electrochemical improvements indicate that the N-GQD coating increased the stability of the SEI layer during repeated Li plating/stripping cycles.

Because the high power capability of LMBs is a major benchmark in real-world applications, we increased the current rates and tested the rate capability of the N-GQD@Cu symmetric cell (Fig. 4 a–c). The overpotential of the cell increased from 1 to 3, 6, and 12 mV as the current density was increased from 1 to 5, 10, and 20 mA cm−2, respectively. Thereafter, the cell was cycled for more than 9,000 h at a current density of 20 mA cm−2 to deliver a capacity of 1 mAh cm−2 and exhibited remarkable cycle stability. After 9,000 h of cycling, the overpotential was ∼12.6 mV, which displays the exceptional stability of the Li host–electrolyte interface. Conversely, the Cu foam symmetric cell became unstable merely after 8 h of cycling under the same experimental conditions. Furthermore, the N-GQD@Cu symmetric cell exhibited excellent capacity retention for 700 h (Fig. 4d), measured at an areal capacity of 5 mAh cm−2 and a current density of 20 mA cm−2. Moreover, voltage profiles of N-GQD@Cu symmetric cells given in Fig. 4a and d are zoomed-in and presented in Fig. S5a and b, respectively. As it is clearly evident from the zoomed-in voltage profiles, no fluctuation in voltage was observed even after 9,000 h of cycles at 20 mA cm−2 to a capacity of 1 mAh cm−2. The absence of voltage fluctuations indicated that no short circuit occurred in the symmetric cells. Furthermore, even when the capacity was increased to 5 mAh cm−2 at a current density of 20 mA cm−2, no voltage fluctuation was observed up to 700 h of cycling. In addition, optical images of symmetric cells after cycling (Fig. S5c) exhibited no evidence of a short circuit.

Inspired by the exceptional reversibility of the N-GQD@Cu symmetric cell with an areal capacity of 1 mAh cm−2, we evaluated its performance at high current rate of 30 mA cm−2 and demonstrated that the cell exhibited stable cycling performance for over 8,000 h (Fig. 4e). This result demonstrated a significant leap in rate performance and durability in comparison to other state-of-the-art strategies in LMBs. Therefore, we conclude that the simple N-GQD coating process significantly improved the cell performance at high current rates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Understanding the effect of N-GQD seed-coating
ce:section.ce:para: The cross-section of the N-GQD@Cu morphology and SEI layer (Li plating of 1 mAh cm−2 at 1 mA cm−2) was characterized by focused ion beam-transmission electron microscopy (FIB-TEM). The electrode image revealed the presence of two layers on the Cu foam substrate (Fig. 5 a). The top layer showed the uniform distribution of N-GQDs, within a matrix assigned to be the SEI layer. Because the SEI layer is generally comprised of elements with relatively higher electron densities than Li, the top dark-colored layer in the FIB-TEM image corresponded to the N-GQD-intermixed SEI layer, whereas the bottom layer represented Li deposits. The thicknesses of the composite SEI and deposited Li metal layer were 80 and 50 nm, respectively. Li metal deposition occurred between the composite SEI layer and Cu foam electrode, leading to the formation of Cu foam/Li/N-GQD SEI stacks. We hypothesized that the N-GQD coating directed the homogenous deposition of Li on the Cu foam surface owing to the high lithiophilicity and insulative properties.

In addition, SEM image and corresponding EDS profiles confirmed that Li was indeed deposited beneath the composite SEI layer. This is especially evident from the area where the composite SEI layer was peeled off during SEM sample preparation (Fig. S6a-c). The corresponding Cu, C and N elemental mappings (Fig. S6d-f) display that the electrolyte was decomposed upon contact with the N-GQD coating layer, resulting in a composite SEI layer. Li deposition was further investigated employing operando optical microscopy and ex-situ SEM analysis. We designed a capillary tube half-cell by assembling an N-GQD-coated flat Cu wire and Li metal (Fig. 5b). For precise optical microscopy observation, the N-GQD layer was coated on one side of the flat Cu wire, and the coating thickness was intentionally increased. Upon applying a current density of 5 mA cm−2, Li deposits formed between the composite SEI layer and Cu wire (Fig. 5c). We recorded a video of the Li deposition process, highlighting the uniform Li filling beneath the composite SEI layer accommodated by the N-GQD coating (Supplementary Video). In addition, the top-view SEM image of the composite SEI layer revealed a uniform morphology and the absence of Li dendrites (Fig. S7). Both the operando and ex-situ analyses results confirmed that Li deposited on the Cu foam surface was passivated by the composite SEI layer.

The formation of composite SEI layer was further verified by XPS analysis at different etching times (Fig. 5d-e). Particularly, we focused on the Li 1 s and F 1 s XPS profiles because LiF is one of the key components of the SEI layer[50]. The peak at 684.8 eV in the F 1 s XPS profile of the SEI layer was attributed to LiF, and the intensity of the peak was inversely proportional to the etching time. Furthermore, an additional peak at 688.4 eV assigned to C-F group disappeared with further etching[26,30]. Moreover, the intensity of the LiF peak at 55.7 eV in the Li 1 s XPS profile of the SEI layer presented a similar trend to that of the aforementioned peak in the F 1 s XPS profile of the SEI layer (Fig. 5d). The decreasing trends of LiF and C-F groups were ascribed to the etching of the SEI layer. These results indicated that the top surface was comprised of C-F containing molecules, and LiF was the predominant SEI layer component beneath it. These results are well matched with the EDS analysis, indicating that the SEI layer comprised N-GQDs and other components, such as LiF.

Aiming to achieve the long-term electrochemical stability of LMBs, we therefore investigated the robustness of the composite SEI layer during repetitive volume changes. Since the Li deposition occurred beneath the SEI layer, the volume expansion associated with Li plating would exert outward pressure on the SEI layer. Such stress may result in the breakage of SEI layer at a certain capacity limit and lead to accelerated dendrite formation and lead to early cell failure. Ex-situ electrodes were examined by cross-sectional SEM analysis after Li deposition up to 7 mAh cm−2 (Fig. S8). N-GQD@Cu showed a smooth surface after 2 mAh cm−2 of Li deposition, implying that SEI layer was stable under the pressure exerted by volume expansion (Fig. S8a-b). When the capacity was increased to 7 mAh cm−2 (Fig. S8c-d), smooth lithium plating morphology was observed without any noticeable dendrite growth in N-GQD@Cu. In contrast, Cu foam exhibited a highly dendritic structure at 7 mAh cm−2 capacity (Fig. S8e-f). These ex-situ results suggest that uniform Li plating in N-GQD@Cu exerted even stress distribution throughout the composite SEI layer. In the case of the Cu foam, the irregular Li plating is generated on account of uneven pressure on the SEI layer followed by the breakage of the layer. Moreover, we postulate that the induced stress was compensated by the re-arrangement of N-GQDs in the composite SEI layer enabled by their weaker inter-particle interactions[51,52].

Considering the potential practical applications of LMBs, we assembled a full cell using pre-lithiated N-GQD@Cu electrodes with LiFePO4 (LFP) cathode (Fig. 6 a–c). The N/P ratio of the electrodes was 2 and the full cell was pre-cycled at a rate of 0.1C for three cycles in the voltage range of 2.0–4.0 V. The capacity of the cell after five cycles at the rates of 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, and 5.0C, was 141, 135, 131, 124, and 107 mAh g−1, respectively (Fig. 6a). When the rate was increased from 0.1 to 5.0C, the cell exhibited capacity retention of 78%, which was higher than the initial capacity of the Cu foam electrodes-based cell (64%). Subsequently, we continuously measured the cycling performance of the N-GQD@Cu/LFP cell at a rate of 1.0C. The cell displayed exceptional cycling stability, retaining 80% of the initial capacity after 500cycles (Fig. 6b). These results further support our observations that N-GQD@Cu aided the formation of a stable SEI layer, thereby inhibiting the continuous consumption of the electrolyte. By comparison, the cell consisting of Cu foam/LFP electrodes presented a sudden decrease in capacity before 100 cycles and highly fluctuating CE values.

In summary, our findings provide key insights towards realizing a flawless electrochemical deposition of Li metal on the host surface at high current densities. The lithiophilic N-GQD seed-coating offers electrically insulating and good ion conducting properties at the nanoscale. Because of these unique material properties, lithium nucleation and plating predominantly occurred at the SEI layer–Cu foam interface. Moreover, homogeneous Li plating and mechanical robustness of the composite SEI layer prevented continuous consumption of electrolytes and resulted in ultra-long cycling stability even at high current densities. This study contributes to the current knowledge of Li metal anodes by suggesting that a simple seed-coating method is a promising strategy for achieving high power and long cycle life LMBs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIP) (No. 2020R1A2B5B02002247), and the Technology Innovation Program (No. 20012341) funded by the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MOTIE, Korea). Dong Jun Kim acknowledges the support from the Australian Research Council (DE210101618).
